title: Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa: The Living Science of Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the stream that flows near our college), learning how our tūpuna monitored water quality through observation of native species like kōura (freshwater crayfish) and kākahi (freshwater mussels), and connecting this mātauranga to modern water quality science.
learningObjectives: Measure and record water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, temperature) using scientific instruments and compare with traditional Ngāti Kahungunu indicators.
Explain the relationship between specific macroinvertebrate species (kōura, kākahi) and water health, linking traditional knowledge to ecological concepts.
Create a collaborative data visualization that integrates traditional observations with modern measurements to tell the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Propose a culturally-informed action plan to address an identified water quality issue affecting the stream.

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Introduction to Our Stream
activities.description: Begin with a karakia and waiata for the awa. Local kaumātua shares pūrākau (traditional stories) about Mangakōtukutuku stream and the significance of its health. Students create a collaborative map annotating key features, historical sites, and potential testing locations.

activities.title: He Tohu o Te Taiao: Traditional Indicators Workshop
activities.description: Hands-on session identifying native macroinvertebrates (kōura, kākahi, mayfly larvae) from samples. Students learn the traditional Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge about what the presence/absence of these species indicates about water health and record observations in a field journal.

activities.title: Ngā Ine Pūtaiao: Modern Water Quality Testing
activities.description: At the stream site, students work in small groups to use water testing kits to scientifically measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature. They compare their quantitative data with their qualitative observations of traditional indicators.

activities.title: Te Hōhonutanga: Data Analysis and Korero
activities.description: Back in the classroom, groups pool their data. They analyze the correlation between the scientific measurements and the presence of sensitive species. A guided discussion explores why both knowledge systems are valuable and how they can be used together.

activities.title: Te Mahi Ngātahi: Action Planning for the Awa
activities.description: Based on their findings, student groups develop a brief 'Awa Ora' (Healthy Stream) action plan. This plan must propose one action rooted in mātauranga Māori (e.g., planting harakeke to filter runoff) and one using modern science (e.g., monitoring nitrate levels over time) to address an issue they identified.

resources: Local Kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
LAWA (Land, Air, Water Aotearoa) website's 'Can I Swim Here?' and 'Water Quality' data pages
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) identification guides
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's environmental education resources and water testing kits

tasks: Completed field journal entries with sketches, traditional observations, and scientific measurements.
Group data analysis sheet showing the comparison between traditional indicators and scientific measurements.
Presentation of the 'Awa Ora' action plan to the class and invited kaumātua, justifying the proposed actions using evidence from both knowledge systems.

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science - Students will learn about science as a way of investigating the natural world and how it can be integrated with other knowledge systems.
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interaction with other Earth systems.
The NZ Curriculum Principle of the Treaty of Waitangi - Exploring Māori and Pākehā perspectives on environmental management.
